coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots from succinic acid and glycerol as ferrous ion and hydrogen peroxide dual-mode sensors and for cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Prathumsuwan, Thitarat
Jamnongsong, Supawan
Sampattavanich, Somponnat
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots are optically unique materials that have many applications, including photocatalysis, drug delivery, and biosensing. In this work, carbon dots were synthesized in high yields from succinic acid and glycerol using a green, one-step hydrothermal method, and without the addition of acid or base catalysts. Blue- and green-fluorescence could be selected for simply by varying the reaction time, at quantum efficiencies of 11% and 7%, respectively. The blue-fluorescent carbon dots were tested as dual-mode sensors for the detection of ferrous ion (Fe2+) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). They were shown to be selective to Fe2+ and H2O2 in solutions, with limits of detection of 21.9 and 0.7 μM, respectively. The carbon dots were used to prepare paper-based sensors that can be used to determine Fe2+ and H2O2 concentrations as low as 30 μM and 400 nM, respectively. Fe2+ and H2O2 in several real water samples were determined with excellent recovery and repeatability. They were also demonstrated to be luminescent in the polymeric materials, giving them a wide range of potential solid-state applications. In the tests of cell imaging, they were shown to have excellent biocompatibility, with cell viability of 90% at carbon dot concentrations of up to 1 g L−1. Our carbon dots are thus suitable for use as chemical and intracellular probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioimaging
Carbon dot
Nanoparticle
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
Metal ion and hydrogen peroxide detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of carbon dots from succinic acid and glycerol via hydrothermal treatment, images under UV illumination, and applications.

(a) TEM image and (b) size distribution of the blue-emitting dots (c) TEM image and (d) the size distribution of the green-emitting dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
FT-IR spectra of blue- and green-emitting carbon dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) XPS survey spectra and (b) high-resolution of the C1s signal of blue-emitting carbon dots, (c) XPS survey spectra and (d) high-resolution of the C1s signal of green-emitting carbon dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Absorbance and photoluminescence spectra of (a) the blue-emitting and (b) green-emitting dots in aqueous solution. Insets show photographs of the carbon dots under visible light (left) and UV light (right). (c) Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the blue-emitting and (d) green-emitting dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) blue-emitting (b) green-emitting dots in aqueous solution at different pH values. Plots of fluorescent intensity against pH of (c) blue-emitting and (d) green-emitting dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots and H2O2 in the presence of metal ions, (b) relative fluorescent intensity of carbon dot/H2O2 solutions containing different metal ions (λex = 280 nm, [Mn+] = 1000 ppm). The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (c) The carbon dot/H2O2-coated filter paper in the presence of metal ions, taken under UV light.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dot/H2O2 solutions in the presence of Fe2+ (from bottom to top: 0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 1000 μM). (b) Stern-Volmer plots of fluorescence emission intensity and Fe2+ concentration. Error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (c) Carbon dot/H2O2-coated filter in the presence of Fe2+ taken under UV light. (d) Plot of RGB values and Fe2+ concentrations of carbon dot/H2O2-coated filter paper under UV illumination. Three spots were taken for the calculation of average values.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dot solutions in the presence of H2O2 (from top to bottom: 0, 100, 200 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, and 1000 nM). (b) Stern-Volmer plots of fluorescence emission intensity and H2O2 concentration. The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. (c) Carbon dot coated filter in the presence of H2O2, taken under UV light. (d) Plot of RGB values and H2O2 concentrations of carbon dot-coated filter paper under UV illumination. Three spots were taken for the calculation of average values.
(a) Photograph of the carbon dot/PMMA film under the UV light and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of PMMA film and carbon dot/PMMA nanocomposite film (λex = 280 nm).

(a) Relative viability of human bile duct cancer (KKU213) cells treated with carbon dots in the range 0.00–2.00 g L−1 for 24 h, Subcellular localization of carbon dots inside the human bile duct cancer (KKU213) cells. Fluorescent images of control (no carbon dots) (b) and 0.75 mg per well carbon dots (c). (d–g) Images showing subcellular localization of carbon dots inside the human bile duct cancer (KKU213) cells, including fluorescent image of NLS-mCherry protein (d), bright field overlaid with both NLS-mCherry protein and carbon dots (e), fluorescence image of carbon dots (f) and overlaid images of carbon dots and NLS-mCherry protein (g).
Percent yield of carbon dots from previous studies and this work.
Detection limits of carbon dots for Fe2+ detection from previous studies and this work.
Detection limits of carbon dots for H2O2 detection from previous studies and this work.
Cell viability of carbon dots from previous studies and this work.
Determination of H2O2 recovery in carbon dot solutions prepared from real water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All experimental results were obtained based on five independent measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Fe2+ recovery in carbon dot solutions prepared from real water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All experimental results were obtained based on five independent measurements.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots from succinic acid and glycerol as ferrous ion and hydrogen peroxide dual-mode sensors and for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology, Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, 2 Prannok Road, Bangkoknoi, Bangkok, 10700, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots are optically unique materials that have many applications, including photocatalysis, drug delivery, and biosensing. In this work, carbon dots were synthesized in high yields from succinic acid and glycerol using a green, one-step hydrothermal method, and without the addition of acid or base catalysts. Blue- and green-fluorescence could be selected for simply by varying the reaction time, at quantum efficiencies of 11% and 7%, respectively. The blue-fluorescent carbon dots were tested as dual-mode sensors for the detection of ferrous ion (Fe2+) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). They were shown to be selective to Fe2+ and H2O2 in solutions, with limits of detection of 21.9 and 0.7 μM, respectively. The carbon dots were used to prepare paper-based sensors that can be used to determine Fe2+ and H2O2 concentrations as low as 30 μM and 400 nM, respectively. Fe2+ and H2O2 in several real water samples were determined with excellent recovery and repeatability. They were also demonstrated to be luminescent in the polymeric materials, giving them a wide range of potential solid-state applications. In the tests of cell imaging, they were shown to have excellent biocompatibility, with cell viability of 90% at carbon dot concentrations of up to 1 g L−1. Our carbon dots are thus suitable for use as chemical and intracellular probes.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are unique fluorescent nanoparticles whose size-dependent physical and optical properties, originate from the quantum confinement effect [1]. These nanometer-sized and fluorescent QDs are used in many applications, including sensing [2,3], optoelectronics [4–6], and bioimaging [7–11]. QDs, however, have several drawbacks, limiting their practical uses. For example, QDs generally contain toxic heavy metals, such as cadmium and lead, making them unsuitable in biomedical applications [12]. In addition, QDs are hydrophobic with low solubility in aqueous solutions, and surface modification with appropriate ligands is necessary to improve their solubility [13]. Development of water-soluble and biocompatible fluorescent nanoparticles is a research goal.

Carbon quantum dots or carbon dots are carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials that are used as an alternative to conventional toxic metal-based QDs. They were discovered in 2004 by Xu et al. as fragments during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes from carbon soot using gel electrophoresis [14]. Over the past decade, hundreds of studies of carbon dots have been reported. They offer several advantages over conventional QDs, including simpler synthesis, lower cost of precursors, higher biocompatibility, and higher water solubility without the use of organic ligands [15]. Carbon dots can be prepared using two approaches: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down approach involves breaking down large carbon structures, such as graphite [16–19], carbon nanotubes [20], or activated carbon [21], using methods that include arc-discharge [22], laser-ablation [23], and electrochemical oxidation [24]. In contrast, the bottom-up approaches are based on hydrolysis, polymerization, or carbonization reactions. These can be performed using chemical precursors, natural precursors, wastes, or other carbon-rich materials, by applying combustion/thermal treatment or microwave irradiation, often with the aid of acid or base catalysts [25]. Bottom-up methods are therefore more convenient and more cost-effective for producing carbon dots in high yields. The obtained carbon dots are highly water-soluble due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylate. The abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the surface also make carbon dots highly selective to adsorption of chemicals and metal ions. Because of their optical properties, carbon dots are widely used as fluorescent sensors for chemicals and metal ions, such as Hg2+ [26], Fe3+ [27], Cu2+ [28], Pt4+ [29], thrombin [30,31], glucose [32–34], dopamine [35,36], nucleic acid [37], phosphate [38], and nitrite [39]. The tunable fluorescence, biocompatibility, and high photostability of carbon dots make them suitable for bioimaging applications. They have been used as a staining probe for imaging a variety of living cells, including breast cancer (MDA-MB 468) cells [40], human melanoma A375 cells [41], human colon adenocarcinoma (HT-29) cells [42], A2780 [43], CAL-27 [44], NIH 3T3 [45], MDCK and HeLa cells [46].

In this work, carbon dots were prepared from succinic acid and glycerol using a one-step hydrothermal method. Succinic acid and glycerol are biocompatible carbon- and oxygen-rich building blocks in chemical synthesis, and are commonly used for the synthesis of step polymers and as ingredients in food and supplement products [47,48]. They are relatively cheap and can be produced from renewable resources. Blue- and green-fluorescent carbon dots were prepared without the use of acid, base, or organic solvent, offering an environmentally less damaging and economical approach. The carbon dots were characterized using UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), zeta potential measurement, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The use of the carbon dots as dual-mode fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of Fe2+ and H2O2 was investigated. Only a very limited number of studies have reported Fe2+ and H2O2 sensors based on carbon dots [39,49–53]. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to report paper-based carbon dot sensors for detection of Fe2+ and H2O2. Iron comprises up to five percent of the Earth's crust. It is found in ground water, where it degrades water quality, and is used in a wide range of industries. Iron is beneficial to living organisms; however, too much intake or exposure can cause several health problems. Meanwhile, H2O2 is an oxidizing and sterilizing agent in many consumer products and an important by-product from oxidase-based enzymatic reactions. H2O2 can be harmful to living organisms. Interference experiments and determination of Fe2+ and H2O2 in real water samples were carried out to demonstrate the practicality of our methods. The carbon dots were also investigated for cell imaging and preparation of fluorescent plastics with poly(methyl methacrylate), giving them a wide range of potential solid-state applications. The one-step hydrothermal method used in this work represents a novel green strategy for the preparation of carbon dots as chemical sensors and bio-imaging agents.

All chemicals were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received without further purification. Cellulose dialysis membrane (1000 Da MWCO) was bought from Membrane Filtration Product, Inc. Deionized (DI) water (R > 18 MΩ cm−1) was used in all experiments.

In a typical one-step hydrothermal synthesis, succinic acid (1.25 g) was dissolved in deionized water (25 mL), followed by the addition of glycerol (25 mL). The mixture was then sonicated for 15 min, poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave, and heated at 250 °C for 6 h. When the reaction was complete, the reactor was cooled to room temperature (30 °C). The brown-black and transparent mixture was diluted with deionized water (30 mL) and neutralized using potassium hydroxide (0.1 M) and nitric acid (0.5 M). The neutral solution was then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 MWCO) for 72 h. The resulting light-yellow solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove agglomerated particles. Finally, carbon dots were obtained after freeze drying for 24 h as a solid powder (25% blue-emitting and 1% green-emitting).

The quantum yield (
∅
X
) of the carbon dots were measured based on the slope method using following equation:
(1)
∅
X
=
∅
ST
(
Grad
X
Grad
ST
)
(
η
X
2
η
ST
2
)
where Grad is the gradient (slope) from the plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance, and
η
is the refractive index of the solution. The subscripts ST and X denote the quinine sulfate and carbon dot aqueous solutions, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as a standard (ϕ ST = 54% at 360 nm).

The sensitivity of carbon dots to different concentrations of H2O2 was examined. The carbon dot (0.05 g L−1) solution was prepared and mixed with the various concentrations of H2O2 (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, and 1000 nM) at a 1:1 vol ratio. After 30 min, the fluorescence of all solutions was recorded using 280 nm excitation.

Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cr2+, Mn2+, Ti2+, Mg2+ and Ag+ ions were chosen to test the selectivity of the carbon dots. The carbon dots (0.01 g) were dispersed in DI water (1.0 L) and mixed with H2O2 solution (600 μM) at a 1:1 vol ratio. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 3.0 using 1 M H2SO4 and the fluorescent intensity was defined as F0. Next, metal solutions (5.0 mL, 1000 ppm) and carbon dot/H2O2 solutions (5.0 mL) were mixed and transferred to a quartz cuvette. The fluorescence emission spectra were then recorded using 280 nm excitation and the intensity was defined as F.

The sensitivity of the carbon dots to different concentrations of metal ions was examined. Concentrations of 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 1000 μM were prepared and mixed with the carbon dot/H2O2 solutions at a 1:1 vol ratio. After 30 min, the fluorescent intensity of all solutions was recorded using 280 nm excitation.

Real water samples were employed to demonstrate the practicality of carbon dots as Fe2+ and H2O2 sensors. Tap water was collected from our laboratory after water was flowed for 5 min and the river water sample was taken from local pond (Pathumthani, Thailand). Tap water, river water, mineral water (Minéré® bottled water), and drinking water (Crystal® bottled water) were filtered to remove the suspended particles. All water samples were spiked with H2O2 solution at 0.1, 1, 10 μM at a 1:1 vol ratio. The fluorescence emission spectra of all solutions were recorded using 280 nm excitation after incubation for 30 min. The Fe2+ solution (500 μM) was then added to all of mixtures. After 30 min, the fluorescence emission spectra of all solutions were measured using 280 nm excitation.

The recovery and relative standard deviation in percentage were calculated using the following equations:
%
Recovery
=
Concentration
of
analyte
found
Concentration
of
analyte
added
× 100
%
RSD =
SD
X ¯
× 100
where SD is the standard deviation (n = 5) and
X ¯
is averaged concentration (n = 5).

For the cell viability and imaging experiments, human bile duct cancer (KKU213) cells, with and without the nuclear marker NLS (nuclear localization signal)-mCherry, were chosen. Cells were placed in the wells of a microtiter plate at a ratio of 8000 cells per well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2. The cell-culture media (150 μL per well) were then replaced. Carbon dot solution was prepared in a sterile 1X PBS solution (2 g L−1), passed through a syringe filter (0.45 μm), ultrasonicated for 30 min at room temperature. 100 μL of the solution was then added to the first row of wells and diluted two-fold at each of the sequence of eight rows, to obtain carbon dot concentrations of 1.000, 0.500, 0.250, 0.125, 0.0625, 0.0313, 0.0156, and 0.0078 g L−1. The last row of wells as a control contained only cells and culture media without carbon dot solution. The cells were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2, after which the culture media and carbon dot solution were removed. The incubated cells were washed three times with 1X PBS (200 μL). The incubated cells were then imaged (Operetta, 60X high NA objective lens) using 475 nm excitation wavelength and a 525 nm emission wavelength. The graph of relative viability against carbon dot concentrations (0–2 g L−1) was plotted from cell counting.

Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), prepared via classical free radical polymerization, was combined with the carbon dots to prepare a fluorescent plastic. PMMA (5.0 g) in acetone (20 mL) and carbon dots (5.0 g) dissolved in DI water (10 mL) were mixed and poured into a glass mold. The mixture was then heated at 70 °C to obtain a fluorescent film. The film was irradiated with UV light and its image was recorded.

The FT-IR spectra of the carbon dots were obtained using a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum 2000) and KBr pellets. The carbon dots were placed on a glass slide, and the surface elements were investigated using XPS (AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos Analytical, Manchester UK). TEM images were obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope. The average size of the carbon dots was determined using ImageJ software. The samples were prepared by deposition of the carbon dot aqueous solution (1 g L−1) onto a copper grid coated with an ultra-thin film of carbon, and the solvent was removed by evaporation at 60 °C. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence emission spectra of the aqueous solutions (1 g L−1) were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec), and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jasco, FP-6200), respectively. The zeta potential of the carbon dots in aqueous solution (1 g L−1) was measured at 25 °C and an electrode voltage of 0.9 V using a Nano Particle Analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, size, and nanostructure of the carbon dots were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images showed the carbon dots to be uniform in size and spherical in shape. The size distribution suggested that the blue and green carbon dots had an average size of about 2.3 and 4.6 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 ). This is consistent with previous reports that the photoluminescence emission wavelength of a carbon dot depend on its size [58–60], as the green carbon dots were larger than the blue ones. We concluded that a longer reaction time led to more carbonization and consequently larger-sized carbon dots.

To investigate the optical properties of carbon dots, the UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra were obtained (Fig. 5 ). The absorption spectra exhibited a broad peak at 260 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗ transition of an aromatic sp2 system from multiple-aromatic chromophores [67]. The shoulder peak at 345 nm was attributed to the n-π∗ transition of the C=O bond, confirming the presence of carbonyl groups [68]. The blue and green fluorescent carbon dots exhibited strongest emission peaks at 410 and 525 nm, respectively. Furthermore, upon increasing the excitation wavelength from 280 nm to 480 nm, the emission peaks of the carbon dots apparently shifted toward longer wavelengths, indicating excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 5c and d). This observation was attributed to the difference in particle size and various emissive sites on the surfaces of the carbon dots [69]. Such excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior is useful in multicolor imaging applications. The fluorescent quantum yields of blue- and green-emitting dots were 11% and 7%, respectively. The lower quantum yield of the green emitting dots was consistent with previous report [59].

The pH dependence of the fluorescence of the carbon dots in solution was next investigated. This was highest for both types at a pH of 14 (Fig. 6 ), confirming that the carbon dots exhibit strong fluorescence in basic conditions. This may reflect negative charging of the carbon dot particles, which would prevent agglomeration through electrostatic repulsion. The emission intensity of the carbon dots was lower in acidic conditions. The −2.2 mV zeta potential of the carbon dots corresponded to a slightly negative charge on the surface functional groups in aqueous solution. When the solution was acidic, the negatively charged carbon dots became protonated, removing negative charges and electrostatic repulsion, encouraging particle agglomeration, and producing a reduction in fluorescence intensity. These findings confirmed those from previous studies of carbon dots prepared from glucose-glutathione [70], citric acid [44], and 4-formylphenylboronic acid/polyethyleneimine [71], which showed pH-dependent fluorescence emission intensities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion and hydrogen peroxide detection
ce:section.ce:para: As the blue-emitting carbon dots had a higher quantum yield and product yield, they were selected for testing in metal ion sensing and bio-imaging applications. First, to determine their selectivity to metal ions, the fluorescence emission intensity of the carbon dots was measured in the presence of metal ions, including Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cr2+, Mn2+, Ti2+, Mg2+, and Ag+ (1000 ppm). These were chosen as they are widely present in the environment, food, and consumer products. Pollution by metal ions is a major issue, and therefore, detection of metal ions using simple, effect methods is highly desired. First, the carbon dot and metal ion solutions were mixed and subjected to the fluorescence emission measurement. The fluorescence emission intensity of carbon dots was quenched by 60% by the addition of Fe2+ and Cu2+. A modified procedure was then applied by adding H2O2 to improve the selectivity of carbon dots based on a previous report by J. Shi et al. [53] When H2O2 was added to the carbon dot solutions and the mixture was acidified, fluorescence emission was found to be quenched by more than 90% (Fig. 7 a and b). This may be due to photoinduced charge transfer between the carbon dots and the strongly oxidant H2O2, as suggested by previous studies [52]. It was found that the fluorescence emission intensity recovered by approximately 30% when Fe2+ solution was added to the carbon dot/H2O2 mixture. The other metal ions had no significant effect on the fluorescent intensity of the mixture. This is a well-known Fenton reaction that Fe2+ and H2O2 react to form Fe3+, thereby reducing the amount of H2O2 in the solution and consequently producing turn-on fluorescence emission [53]. As the addition of Fe3+ ions did not affect the fluorescent intensity of the carbon dots, our carbon dots were able to effectively distinguish between Fe2+ and Fe3+. The selectivity of carbon dots to Fe2+ was further tested by loading onto Whatman cellulose-based filter paper. The paper was immersed in 0.1 g L−1 carbon dot/H2O2 solution at pH = 3. After 3 min, the paper was removed and dried at room temperature. The metal ion test solutions (1000 μM) were then individually dropped onto the coated filter paper, which was placed under a UV lamp. The images shown in Fig. 7c were then captured. As can be seen, the carbon dot/H2O2-coated filter paper showed no fluorescence, in contrast with pure carbon dot-coated paper. This was attributed to quenching of fluorescence emission by the H2O2. However, after Fe2+ solution was added, the fluorescence emission of the carbon dot/H2O2-coated paper recovered, becoming visible to the naked eye. This demonstrated turn-off fluorescence upon addition of H2O2, and turn-on when Fe2+ was added. This dual-mode, portable paper-based device suggests practical uses in Fe2+ and H2O2 sensing applications.

To demonstrate the photoluminescent properties of the carbon dots in solid state, they were next used as the fluorophore in plastic. This is crucial for various optoelectronics applications. The transparent plastic poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was chosen as it is a used in a wide range of products. Carbon dot/PMMA nanocomposite film was fabricated and examined under UV light (Fig. 10 a). The fluorescence spectrum of carbon dot/PMMA nanocomposite was blue-shifted, compared to that of carbon dot solution, due to a change in surrounding environment (Fig. 10b) [72]. The emission spectra of the film resembled that of pure carbon dots, further confirming that the nanocomposite was fluorescent when in a solid state. The carbon dots were shown to homogeneously disperse in the PMMA, suggesting uses as an alternative fluorescent dye.

To investigate the potential use of carbon dots as optical labels in cell imaging and biomedical applications, human bile duct cancer cells were incubated in the presence of the carbon dots. First, cell viability in the carbon dot solution was determined to test the biocompatibility of the carbon dots. Cell viability was found to exceed 90% at carbon dot concentrations up to 1 g L−1 (Fig. 11 a). This is higher than the previously reported values (Table 4). The carbon dots were then tested as fluorescent probes in cell imaging. The fluorescent image of cells incubated with carbon dots showed the green and bright fluorescence after 24 h incubation, compared with dark image from cells with no carbon dots (control) (Fig. 11b and c). This result suggested that carbon dots could be localized inside the cells. The localization of carbon dots inside cells was further investigated by utilizing the human bile duct cancer (KKU213) cells, containing mCherry as the nuclear marker. After incubating for 24 h, the washed cells were imaged using high content screening under UV light (λex = 475 nm, λem = 525 nm) and under a bright field. The carbon dots were demonstrated to have entered and labeled the cells, in particular the cytoplasm (Fig. 11d–g). This is consistent with previous studies, which reported primary localization of the negatively charged carbon dots in the cell cytoplasm, rather than in the nucleus [73,74]. This suggests that our carbon dots are promising candidates as in vivo bioimaging probes for diagnostic use.

This study demonstrated the production of multi-colored, highly fluorescent, and water-soluble carbon dots at high yields from succinic acid and glycerol, using a one-step hydrothermal method and without requiring an acid or base reagent. Blue- or green-emitting dots could be selected simply by varying the reaction time. Our carbon dots exhibited turn-off fluorescence upon addition of H2O2, and turn-on fluorescence upon addition of Fe2+. The carbon dots were demonstrated to act as sensitive and selective Fe2+ ion and H2O2 sensors with limits of detection as low as 21.9 and 0.7 μM, respectively. For the first time, paper-based sensors were fabricated, demonstrating a practical method of utilizing the carbon dots to detect Fe2+ and H2O2 and in micromolar and sub-micromolar concentrations, respectively. It was found that the cell viability of 90% at carbon dot concentrations of up to 1 g L−1. The low cytotoxicity of the carbon dots will allow them to be used as bioimaging probes. They were shown to display strong, bright fluorescence when absorbed by cells. The carbon dot/PMMA composite exhibited fluorescence when in a solid state. The multifunctional carbon dots developed in this work have a range of potential applications in sensing, bioimaging, and optoelectronics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a governmental budget through Thammasat University in the fiscal year 2017 (Contract No. 6/2560). Prathumsuwan is funded by a Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand. The authors acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University. In addition, the authors would like to thank Mr. John Winward for English language proofing.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Beyond labels: a review of the application of quantum dots as integrated components of assays, bioprobes, and biosensors utilizing optical transduction
